rules: Variables must be defined

subtopics.title: Variables
subtopics.definition: Variables are used to store data values in a program.
subtopics.flow: Declare Variable → Assign Value → Use Variable → Output
rules: Variable names must start with a letter or underscore
Cannot start with a number
Case-sensitive
flowcharts: Start → Create Variable → Assign Value → Use → End

examples.code:
x = 10
name = 'John'
print(x, name)
examples.explanation: Stores values in variables and prints them.

important_notes: No need to declare type in Python
Dynamic typing is used

subtopics.definition: Data types define the type of data a variable can hold.
subtopics.flow: Assign Value → Python Detects Type → Use Data
rules: Each value has a type
Type determines operations allowed
flowcharts: Start → Assign Value → Identify Type → Use → End

examples.code:

y = 10.5
z = 'Hello'

examples.explanation: Displays the type of each variable.

important_notes: Python is dynamically typed
No need to declare type explicitly
subtopics.summary: Data types define the nature of data stored.

subtopics.definition: Type casting is converting one data type into another.
subtopics.flow: Original Type → Apply Function → Converted Type
rules: Use int(), float(), str() functions
Invalid conversions cause errors
flowcharts: Start → Take Value → Convert Type → Use → End

examples.code:

print(y + 5)

important_notes: Useful when taking input from user
Be careful with invalid conversions
subtopics.summary: Type casting changes data type for proper operations.

subtopics.flow: Write Code → Add Comments → Run Program
rules: Use # for single-line comments

flowcharts: Start → Write Code → Add Comment → Execute → End

important_notes: Improves code readability
Used for documentation
subtopics.summary: Comments help explain code without affecting execution.

topics.title: Operators
rules: Follow precedence

examples.code: print(5+2)

important_notes: Know operator types

subtopics.title: Arithmetic Operators
subtopics.definition: Arithmetic operators are used to perform mathematical operations on numeric values.
subtopics.flow: Operands → Arithmetic Operator → Result
rules: Follows BODMAS/PEMDAS order of operations
Division (/) always returns a float
Floor division (//) returns an integer
Modulus (%) returns the remainder
flowcharts: Start → Input Values → Apply Operator → Compute Result → Output → End

examples.code: print(10 + 5)
print(10 - 5)
print(10 * 5)
print(10 / 3)
print(10 // 3)
print(10 % 3)
print(2 ** 8)

important_notes: / always returns float even if result is whole number
** has higher precedence than * or /
Use parentheses to control order of operations
subtopics.concept: Python evaluates arithmetic expressions following standard mathematical precedence rules.
subtopics.summary: Arithmetic operators perform basic math operations: +, -, *, /, //, %, **

subtopics.title: Comparison Operators
subtopics.definition: Comparison operators compare two values and return a Boolean result: True or False.
subtopics.flow: Value A → Comparison Operator → Value B → Boolean Result
rules: Always returns True or False
Can compare numbers, strings, and other data types
String comparison is case-sensitive and based on ASCII values
Chaining comparisons is allowed: 1 < x < 10
flowcharts: Start → Input Two Values → Apply Comparison → Return True/False → End

examples.code: print(5 == 5)
print(5 != 3)
print(10 > 3)
print(2 < 1)
print(5 >= 5)
print(4 <= 3)
print('a' == 'a')
print('b' > 'a')
print(type(5 == 5))

important_notes: == checks equality; = is assignment — never confuse them
Comparing different types (e.g., int vs str) may raise TypeError
Chained comparisons like 1 < x < 10 work natively in Python
subtopics.concept: Comparison operators evaluate relationships between values and are heavily used in conditionals and loops.
subtopics.summary: Comparison operators return True or False by comparing two values: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=

subtopics.title: Logical Operators
subtopics.definition: Logical operators combine multiple conditions and return a Boolean result based on logical relationships.
subtopics.flow: Condition A → Logical Operator → Condition B → Boolean Result
rules: and returns True only if both conditions are True
or returns True if at least one condition is True
not reverses the Boolean value
Short-circuit evaluation: and stops at first False, or stops at first True
flowcharts: Start → Evaluate Condition A → Apply Logical Operator → Evaluate Condition B → Return Result → End

tables.title: Logical Operators
rows: Both A and B are True
At least one of A or B is True

examples.code: print(True and True)
print(True and False)
print(False or True)
print(False or False)
print(not True)
print(not False)
print(5 > 3 and 2 < 4)
print(5 > 10 or 2 < 4)
print(not (5 == 5))
print(10 > 5 and 3 != 2 and 'a' == 'a')

important_notes: Python uses short-circuit evaluation for efficiency
Non-boolean values are evaluated as truthy or falsy
0, None, empty string, and empty list are falsy
subtopics.concept: Logical operators combine Boolean expressions and are core to decision-making in programs.
subtopics.summary: Logical operators (and, or, not) combine conditions to control program flow.

subtopics.title: Assignment Operators
subtopics.definition: Assignment operators assign or update the value of a variable, often combining assignment with an arithmetic operation.
subtopics.flow: Variable → Assignment Operator → Value → Updated Variable
rules: = is the basic assignment operator
Compound operators (+=, -=, etc.) modify and reassign in one step
The variable must be declared before using compound operators
Right-hand side is evaluated before assignment
flowcharts: Start → Declare Variable → Apply Assignment Operator → Store Result → End

examples.code: x = 10; print(x)
x = 10; x += 5; print(x)
x = 10; x -= 3; print(x)
x = 10; x *= 2; print(x)
x = 10; x /= 4; print(x)
x = 10; x //= 3; print(x)
x = 10; x %= 3; print(x)
x = 2; x **= 4; print(x)
x = 5; x += x; print(x)

important_notes: += on strings performs concatenation: s += ' world'
Compound operators are shorthand — they don't change behavior
Multiple assignment is possible: a = b = c = 0
subtopics.concept: Assignment operators store values in variables; compound operators update variables efficiently in one step.
subtopics.summary: Assignment operators assign and update variable values: =, +=, -=, *=, /=, //=, %=, **=

subtopics.title: Bitwise Operators
subtopics.definition: Bitwise operators perform operations directly on the binary (bit-level) representation of integers.
subtopics.flow: Integer A → Convert to Binary → Apply Bitwise Operator → Convert to Integer → Result
rules: Operate on binary bits (0s and 1s)
Only applicable to integers
Results are returned as integers
Left shift multiplies by powers of 2; right shift divides by powers of 2
flowcharts: Start → Input Integers → Convert to Bits → Apply Operation Bit by Bit → Convert Result to Integer → Output → End

examples.code: print(5 & 3)
print(5 | 3)
print(5 ^ 3)
print(~5)
print(5 << 1)
print(5 >> 1)
print(bin(5))
print(bin(5 & 3))
print(2 << 3)
print(16 >> 2)

important_notes: ~ flips all bits and returns -(n+1) due to two's complement
Left shift by n = multiply by 2^n
Right shift by n = integer divide by 2^n
Commonly used in low-level programming and encryption
subtopics.concept: Bitwise operators manipulate data at the binary level, enabling efficient low-level computations.
subtopics.summary: Bitwise operators (&, |, ^, ~, <<, >>) work on binary representations of integers.

subtopics.title: Identity & Membership Operators
subtopics.definition: Identity operators check if two variables point to the same object in memory; membership operators check if a value exists within a sequence.
subtopics.flow: Value / Variable → Identity or Membership Operator → Object / Sequence → True or False
rules: is checks object identity (same memory location), not equality
== checks value equality
in checks if a value exists in a sequence
not in checks if a value does not exist in a sequence
flowcharts: Identity: Start → Compare Memory Addresses → Same? → True / False → End
Membership: Start → Search Sequence → Found? → True / False → End

tables.title: Identity Operators
rows: a and b point to the same object
a and b point to different objects

tables.title: Membership Operators
rows: x is found in the sequence
x is not found in the sequence

examples.code: a = [1,2,3]; b = a; print(a is b)
x = None; print(x is None)
a = 5; b = 5; print(a is not b)
print(3 in [1, 2, 3])
print(4 not in [1, 2, 3])
print('a' in 'apple')
print('z' not in 'apple')
print(10 in range(1, 20))

important_notes: Use is only to compare with None, True, or False — not for general equality
Small integers (-5 to 256) are cached in Python, so is may return True unexpectedly
in works on strings, lists, tuples, sets, and dictionaries (checks keys)
subtopics.concept: Identity operators compare memory references; membership operators search for values within sequences.
subtopics.summary: Identity operators (is, is not) check object identity; membership operators (in, not in) check sequence membership.
real_time_examples: Validating user input against allowed options

topics.title: Conditional Statements
topics.definition: Used to make decisions in code.
rules: Indentation required
flowcharts: Start → Condition → Yes/No → End

examples.code:
if a>10:
 print('Big')

important_notes: Used in decision making

subtopics.title: if Statement
subtopics.definition: The if statement executes a block of code only when a specified condition evaluates to True.
subtopics.flow: Condition → True → Execute Block → Continue | Condition → False → Skip Block → Continue
rules: Condition must evaluate to True or False
The code block must be indented (4 spaces or 1 tab)
Colon (:) is mandatory after the condition
If condition is False, the block is skipped entirely
flowcharts: Start → Evaluate Condition → True? → Execute if Block → End | False? → Skip → End
important_notes: Indentation is mandatory — Python raises IndentationError if missing
Any non-zero, non-empty value is treated as True
A single if block has no else — it simply skips if condition is False
subtopics.concept: The if statement is the most basic decision-making tool — it runs a code block only when its condition is True.
subtopics.summary: if executes a code block only when the given condition is True, otherwise skips it.
real_time_examples: Show discount only if user is a member
Send alert only if temperature exceeds limit
Display error only if form field is empty

subtopics.title: if-else Statement
subtopics.definition: The if-else statement executes one block of code when the condition is True, and a different block when it is False.
subtopics.flow: Condition → True → Execute if Block → End | Condition → False → Execute else Block → End
rules: else does not take a condition — it is the default fallback
Exactly one of the two blocks always executes
Both blocks must be properly indented
else must be at the same indentation level as if
flowcharts: Start → Evaluate Condition → True? → if Block → End | False? → else Block → End

examples.code:
score = 85
result = 'Pass' if score >= 50 else 'Fail'
print(result)

important_notes: else cannot have a condition — use elif for additional conditions
The last example shows a one-line ternary form: value_if_true if condition else value_if_false
Only one block executes — never both
subtopics.concept: if-else guarantees that exactly one of two code paths always executes based on the condition's result.
subtopics.summary: if-else runs the if block when True and the else block when False — one always executes.
real_time_examples: Pass or fail result in exams
Light switch ON or OFF logic

subtopics.title: elif Statement
subtopics.definition: The elif (else-if) statement checks multiple conditions in sequence and executes the block of the first condition that is True.
subtopics.flow: Condition 1 → True → Block 1 → End | False → Condition 2 → True → Block 2 → End | False → ... → else Block → End
rules: elif must come after if and before else
Any number of elif blocks can be used
Only the first matching condition's block executes
The final else is optional but acts as a default
flowcharts: Start → Check if → True? → Block 1 → End | False → Check elif → True? → Block 2 → End | False → else Block → End

tables.title: elif Structure
rows: Second check (only if if is False)

important_notes: Only the first True condition's block runs — remaining elif and else are skipped
elif is more efficient than multiple separate if statements
Without else, no block executes if all conditions are False
subtopics.concept: elif allows multi-way branching — Python checks each condition in order and executes only the first matching block.
subtopics.summary: elif chains multiple conditions; only the first True condition's block runs, with else as the optional default.

subtopics.title: Nested if Statement
subtopics.definition: A nested if statement is an if (or if-else/elif) placed inside another if block, allowing multi-level condition checking.
subtopics.flow: Outer Condition → True → Check Inner Condition → True → Inner Block | False → Inner else | Outer False → Outer else
rules: An if statement can be placed inside any if, elif, or else block
Each level of nesting requires an additional level of indentation
Inner conditions are only checked if the outer condition is True
Avoid deep nesting (more than 3 levels) — it reduces readability
flowcharts: Start → Outer Condition True? → Inner Condition True? → Innermost Block → End | Inner False → Inner else → End | Outer False → Outer else → End

tables.title: Nested if Structure
rows: Second check (only if Level 1 is True)
Runs if inner condition is False
Runs if outer condition is False

important_notes: Each nested level adds 4 spaces of indentation — keep nesting shallow for clean code
Inner if only executes when the outer condition is True
Deeply nested code can often be simplified using and/or logical operators or elif
subtopics.concept: Nested if statements enable multi-level decision making where inner conditions only matter when outer conditions are met.
subtopics.summary: Nested if places an if inside another if, enabling layered condition checks where inner logic depends on outer results.
real_time_examples: Login with username then password check
E-commerce discount based on membership and cart value

topics.title: Loops
rules: Avoid infinite loops

examples.code: for i in range(5): print(i)

important_notes: Important for patterns

subtopics.title: for Loop
subtopics.definition: A for loop iterates over a sequence (list, tuple, string, range, etc.) and executes a block of code once for each item in the sequence.
subtopics.flow: Start → Get Next Item from Sequence → Execute Block → More Items? → Yes → Repeat | No → End
rules: The sequence is evaluated once before the loop starts
The loop variable takes the value of each item in order
Colon (:) is mandatory after the for line
The loop body must be indented
range(start, stop, step) is commonly used to loop a fixed number of times
flowcharts: Start → Initialize Sequence → Pick Next Item → Execute Block → Last Item? → No → Loop Back | Yes → End

examples.code:
for i in range(5):
    print(i)

for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)

for ch in 'Python':
    print(ch)

for i in range(1, 11):
    print(i * 2)

total = 0
for i in range(1, 6):
    total += i
print(total)

for i in range(10, 0, -1):
    print(i)

squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
print(squares)

important_notes: range(stop) starts from 0 by default
range(start, stop, step) — stop is exclusive
Use enumerate() to get both index and value: for i, v in enumerate(list)
List comprehension is a compact form of a for loop
subtopics.concept: The for loop iterates over each item in a sequence one by one, executing the loop body for every item until the sequence is exhausted.
subtopics.summary: for loops iterate over sequences (range, list, string, etc.) executing the body once per item.
real_time_examples: Printing all items in a shopping cart
Sending emails to a list of users
Processing each row in a CSV file

subtopics.title: while Loop
subtopics.definition: A while loop repeatedly executes a block of code as long as a given condition remains True.
subtopics.flow: Start → Check Condition → True → Execute Block → Check Condition Again → False → End
rules: The condition is checked before every iteration
If the condition is False initially, the body never executes
The loop variable must be updated inside the loop to avoid infinite loops
Colon (:) is mandatory after the condition
Use break to exit the loop early if needed
flowcharts: Start → Evaluate Condition → True? → Execute Body → Update Variable → Loop Back | False? → End

examples.code:
i = 0
while i < 5:
    print(i)
    i += 1

n = 1
while n <= 10:
    print(n * 3)
    n += 1

num = 100
while num > 0:
    num //= 2
    print(num)

total = 0
i = 1
while i <= 100:
    total += i
    i += 1
print(total)

x = 1
while x <= 5:
    print(x ** 2)
    x += 1

count = 10
while count >= 1:
    print(count)
    count -= 1
print('Go!')

n = 2
while n < 100:
    print(n)
    n *= 2

i = 0
while i < len('Hello'):
    print('Hello'[i])
    i += 1

attempts = 3
while attempts > 0:
    print('Attempts left:', attempts)
    attempts -= 1

important_notes: Always update the loop variable inside the body to prevent infinite loops
Use while True: with a break for menu-driven or event-driven loops
while is preferred when the number of iterations is not known in advance
subtopics.concept: The while loop keeps executing as long as its condition is True — it is condition-driven, not sequence-driven.
subtopics.summary: while loops run repeatedly as long as a condition is True; manual updates prevent infinite loops.
real_time_examples: Game loop running until player quits

subtopics.title: break Statement
subtopics.definition: The break statement immediately terminates the nearest enclosing loop and transfers control to the statement following the loop.
subtopics.flow: Loop Running → Condition Met → break → Exit Loop Immediately → Continue After Loop
rules: break exits only the innermost loop it is placed in
break can be used in both for and while loops
Code after break inside the loop body is never executed
break is usually placed inside an if condition
flowcharts: Start → Loop → Check break Condition → True → Exit Loop → Continue Program | False → Continue Loop → End

tables.title: break in for vs while
rows: for i in range(10): if i==5: break
Exits infinite loop when x is 0

important_notes: break only exits the innermost loop — outer loops continue
Commonly used with while True: to create controlled infinite loops
Overusing break can make loop logic harder to follow
subtopics.concept: break immediately exits the current loop, skipping all remaining iterations and transferring control to the code after the loop.
subtopics.summary: break terminates the nearest loop immediately when a condition is met, skipping remaining iterations.
real_time_examples: Stop searching once target item is found
Exit game loop when player chooses quit
Stop reading file after encountering an error record

subtopics.title: continue Statement
subtopics.definition: The continue statement skips the rest of the current iteration and moves directly to the next iteration of the loop.
subtopics.flow: Loop Running → Condition Met → continue → Skip Remaining Body → Next Iteration → Continue Loop
rules: continue skips only the current iteration — the loop itself continues
Works in both for and while loops
Code placed after continue in the same iteration is never executed
continue is usually placed inside an if condition
flowcharts: Start → Loop → Check continue Condition → True → Skip Rest of Body → Next Iteration | False → Execute Full Body → Next Iteration → End
important_notes: continue does not exit the loop — it only skips the current iteration
In a while loop, make sure the counter is updated before continue or an infinite loop may occur
continue and break can be combined in the same loop
subtopics.concept: continue skips the remaining code in the current iteration and jumps to the next, allowing selective processing within a loop.
subtopics.summary: continue skips the current iteration and moves to the next one, without exiting the loop.
real_time_examples: Skip blank lines when reading a file
Ignore invalid entries in a data list
Skip weekends when scheduling working days

subtopics.title: pass Statement
subtopics.definition: The pass statement is a null operation — it does nothing and is used as a placeholder where a statement is syntactically required but no action is needed yet.
subtopics.flow: Reach pass → Do Nothing → Continue Execution
rules: pass produces no output and performs no action
Used as a placeholder in empty functions, classes, loops, or conditions
Prevents IndentationError when a block is intentionally left empty
Can be replaced with actual code later during development
flowcharts: Start → Reach pass Statement → No Operation → Continue to Next Statement → End

examples.code:
x = 10
if x > 5:
    pass
print('Checked')

class Animal:
    pass
a = Animal()
print(a)

def login():
    # TODO: implement later
    pass
login()

i = 0
while i < 5:
    pass
    i += 1
print('Done')

important_notes: pass is different from a comment — it is an actual statement that satisfies syntax requirements
Commonly used in test-driven development as a stub before writing logic
Unlike continue, pass does not affect iteration — execution continues normally after it
subtopics.concept: pass is a do-nothing placeholder statement that satisfies Python's syntax requirement for a non-empty block during development.
subtopics.summary: pass does nothing — it is a placeholder used where code is syntactically required but not yet written.

subtopics.title: Nested Loops
subtopics.definition: A nested loop is a loop placed inside another loop. The inner loop completes all its iterations for every single iteration of the outer loop.
subtopics.flow: Outer Loop Iteration 1 → Inner Loop runs fully → Outer Loop Iteration 2 → Inner Loop runs fully → ... → Outer Loop Ends
rules: Any loop type (for/while) can be nested inside any other loop type
The inner loop must be indented inside the outer loop
Inner loop executes completely for each iteration of the outer loop
Total iterations = outer loop count × inner loop count
break and continue inside the inner loop affect only the inner loop
flowcharts: Start → Outer Loop Begin → Inner Loop Begin → Execute Inner Body → Inner Loop Done? → No → Repeat Inner | Yes → Next Outer Iteration → Outer Loop Done? → No → Repeat | Yes → End

tables.title: Common Use Cases of Nested Loops

examples.code:
for i in range(5):
    print('*' * (i + 1))

i = 1
while i <= 3:
    j = 1
    while j <= 3:
        print(i * j, end=' ')
        j += 1
    print()
    i += 1

important_notes: Deeply nested loops (3+ levels) significantly reduce performance and readability
break inside the inner loop exits only the inner loop — the outer continues
Use meaningful variable names (row/col instead of i/j) for clarity in complex loops
Time complexity grows multiplicatively: O(n²) for two nested loops over n items
subtopics.concept: Nested loops allow multi-dimensional iteration — the inner loop runs completely for each step of the outer loop, enabling traversal of grids, matrices, and patterns.
subtopics.summary: Nested loops place one loop inside another; the inner loop completes fully for each outer iteration, used for patterns, matrices, and multi-level data.
real_time_examples: Traversing rows and columns of a spreadsheet
Generating all combinations of items (e.g., color × size)

topics.title: Strings
rules: Immutable

examples.code: print('Hello'[0])

important_notes: Strings are immutable

subtopics.title: String Basics
subtopics.definition: A string in Python is a sequence of characters enclosed in single quotes, double quotes, or triple quotes. Strings are immutable, ordered, and iterable.
subtopics.flow: Define String → Store in Variable → Access / Manipulate → Output
rules: Strings can be enclosed in single (' '), double (" "), or triple (''' ''' / """ """) quotes
Strings are immutable — individual characters cannot be changed after creation
Strings are ordered sequences — each character has a fixed position (index)
Escape sequences like \n, \t are used for special characters
String concatenation uses + and repetition uses *
flowcharts: Start → Define String Literal → Assign to Variable → Perform Operation → Output Result → End

examples.code:
s = 'Hello'
print(s)

s = "Python"
print(type(s))

s = '''This is
a multi-line
string'''
print(s)
print('Hello' + ' ' + 'World')
print('Ha' * 3)
print(len('Python'))
print('He said \'Hello\'')
print('Line1\nLine2\nLine3')

name = 'Alice'
print(f'Hello, {name}!')

s = 'Python'
print(type(s), len(s))

important_notes: Strings are immutable — s[0] = 'J' will raise a TypeError
f-strings (f'...{variable}...') are the modern and preferred way to format strings
len() returns the total number of characters including spaces
subtopics.concept: Strings are immutable sequences of characters that support a wide range of operations including concatenation, repetition, slicing, and formatting.
subtopics.summary: Strings are ordered, immutable character sequences defined with quotes; they support +, *, len(), and f-string formatting.

subtopics.title: String Indexing & Slicing
subtopics.definition: Indexing accesses a single character of a string using its position. Slicing extracts a substring using a start, stop, and optional step value.
subtopics.flow: String → Specify Index or Slice → Extract Character / Substring → Output
rules: Indexing starts at 0 from the left (positive indexing)
Negative indexing starts at -1 from the right
Slicing syntax: string[start:stop:step] — stop index is exclusive
Omitting start defaults to 0; omitting stop defaults to end of string
Strings are immutable — indexing can read but not modify characters
flowcharts: Start → Define String → Provide Index/Slice → Python Extracts Character or Substring → Return Result → End

examples.code:
s = 'Python'
print(s[0])

s = 'Python'
print(s[-1])

s = 'Python'
print(s[2:5])

s = 'Python'
print(s[:3])

s = 'Python'
print(s[3:])

s = 'Python'
print(s[::-1])

s = 'Hello World'
print(s[6:])

s = 'abcdefgh'
print(s[::2])

s = 'Python'
print(s[-3:-1])

important_notes: IndexError is raised if the index is out of range
Slicing never raises an IndexError — out-of-range slices return empty string or partial result
s[::-1] is the most Pythonic way to reverse a string
Strings are zero-indexed — first character is at index 0, not 1
subtopics.concept: Indexing retrieves individual characters by position while slicing extracts substrings using start:stop:step notation — both are read-only due to string immutability.
subtopics.summary: Indexing uses s[i] to get one character; slicing uses s[start:stop:step] to extract substrings. Negative indices count from the end.
real_time_examples: Extracting area code from a phone number string
Reversing a string for palindrome check

subtopics.title: String Methods
subtopics.definition: String methods are built-in functions that perform specific operations on strings and return a new string or value. Since strings are immutable, methods always return a new result without modifying the original.
subtopics.flow: String → Call Method → Python Processes → Return New String or Value → Output
rules: String methods are called using dot notation: string.method()
Methods do not modify the original string — they return a new one
Method names are case-sensitive: .upper() not .Upper()
Some methods accept arguments: replace(old, new), find(sub)
Methods can be chained: s.strip().lower().replace(' ', '_')
flowcharts: Start → Define String → Call Method with Dot Notation → Method Processes String → Return New Result → Store or Print → End

examples.code:
s = 'hello world'
print(s.upper())

s = 'PYTHON'
print(s.lower())

s = '  hello  '
print(s.strip())

s = 'I love Java'
print(s.replace('Java', 'Python'))

s = 'apple,banana,cherry'
print(s.split(','))

words = ['Python', 'is', 'fun']
print(' '.join(words))

s = 'hello'
print(s.find('l'))
print(s.count('l'))

s = '  data science  '
print(s.strip().title())

s = 'filename.txt'
print(s.startswith('file'))
print(s.endswith('.txt'))

s = 'Hello World'
print(s.upper().replace(' ', '_').strip())

important_notes: String methods never modify the original — always assign the result to use it: s = s.upper()
find() returns -1 if the substring is not found; index() raises ValueError instead
split() without argument splits on any whitespace and removes empty strings
Method chaining works because each method returns a new string
subtopics.concept: String methods provide powerful built-in tools to transform, search, validate, and format strings — all returning new values due to immutability.
subtopics.summary: String methods (upper, lower, strip, replace, split, join, find, count, etc.) operate on strings via dot notation and always return new results.
real_time_examples: Cleaning user input with strip() and lower()
Validating email format using endswith() and find()

topics.title: Lists
rules: Mutable

examples.code:
