subtopics.title: List Basics
subtopics.definition: A list in Python is an ordered, mutable, and indexed collection that can store multiple items of different data types in a single variable.
subtopics.flow: Define List → Store in Variable → Access / Modify Items → Output
rules: Lists are defined using square brackets [ ]
Items are separated by commas
Lists are ordered — items maintain their insertion order
Lists are mutable — items can be added, removed, or changed after creation
Lists can contain mixed data types: integers, strings, floats, other lists
flowcharts: Start → Define List with [ ] → Assign to Variable → Access or Modify Elements → Output → End

tables.title: List Characteristics
rows: Items can be changed after creation
Same value can appear multiple times

examples.code:
lst = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(lst)

lst = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print(lst)

lst = [1, 'hello', 3.14, True]
print(lst)

lst = [10, 20, 30]
print(lst[0])
print(lst[-1])

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst[1] = 99
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3]
print(len(lst))

lst = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
print(lst.count(3))

important_notes: Lists are zero-indexed — first element is at index 0
Negative indexing is supported: lst[-1] returns the last element
Nested lists (list of lists) are used to represent matrices and tables
len() returns the total number of elements in the list
subtopics.concept: A list is Python's most versatile and commonly used data structure — ordered, mutable, and capable of holding mixed data types including other lists.
subtopics.summary: Lists are ordered, mutable, indexed collections defined with [ ] that allow duplicates and mixed data types.
real_time_examples: Daily task list in a to-do app

subtopics.title: List Methods
subtopics.definition: List methods are built-in functions that operate on lists to add, remove, sort, search, or modify elements. Unlike string methods, list methods modify the list in place.
subtopics.flow: Define List → Call Method using Dot Notation → List is Modified or Value Returned → Output
rules: List methods are called using dot notation: list.method()
Most list methods modify the list in place and return None
append() adds one item; extend() adds multiple items from an iterable
remove() deletes first occurrence; pop() removes by index
sort() sorts in place; sorted() returns a new sorted list
flowcharts: Start → Define List → Call Method → Method Modifies List or Returns Value → Print / Use Result → End

tables.title: List Methods Reference
rows: Removes and returns item at index i
Returns index of first occurrence of x
Sorts list in ascending order in place
Returns a shallow copy of the list

examples.code:
lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst.append(4)
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst.insert(1, 99)
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst.extend([4, 5, 6])
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3, 2]
lst.remove(2)
print(lst)

lst = [10, 20, 30]
popped = lst.pop(1)
print(popped, lst)

lst = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
lst.sort()
print(lst)

lst = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
lst.sort(reverse=True)
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst.reverse()
print(lst)

lst = [1, 2, 3]
copy_lst = lst.copy()
copy_lst.append(99)
print(lst)
print(copy_lst)

lst = ['banana', 'apple', 'cherry']
lst.sort()
print(lst)
print(lst.index('banana'))

important_notes: sort() modifies the original list; use sorted(lst) to get a new sorted list without changing the original
pop() without an argument removes and returns the last item
remove() raises ValueError if the item is not found
copy() creates a shallow copy — nested lists inside are still referenced, not duplicated
subtopics.concept: List methods provide powerful in-place operations to manage list content — adding, removing, sorting, and searching elements efficiently.
subtopics.summary: List methods (append, insert, extend, remove, pop, sort, reverse, copy, index, count) modify or query the list using dot notation.
real_time_examples: Adding items to a shopping cart (append)

subtopics.title: Lists Coding Questions
subtopics.definition: Practice problems that apply list basics and methods to solve real-world and logic-based Python programming challenges.
subtopics.flow: Understand Problem → Plan Logic → Write Code Using List Operations → Test Output → Verify Result
rules: Always check for edge cases: empty list, single element, duplicates
Use built-in list methods wherever possible for clean code
Prefer list comprehension over loops for simple transformations
Avoid modifying a list while iterating over it
Use len(), range(), and enumerate() effectively with lists
flowcharts: Start → Read Problem → Initialize List → Apply Loop / Method / Condition → Compute Result → Print Output → End

tables.title: List Comprehension vs Loop
rows: for i in lst: if i%2==0: res.append(i)
[i for i in lst if i%2==0]

examples.code:
# Find the largest element
lst = [3, 7, 1, 9, 4]
print(max(lst))

# Sum of all elements
lst = [10, 20, 30, 40]
print(sum(lst))

# Count even numbers
lst = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
count = len([x for x in lst if x % 2 == 0])
print(count)

# Remove duplicates
lst = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
unique = list(set(lst))
print(unique)

# Reverse a list without reverse()
lst = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
print(lst[::-1])

# Find second largest
lst = [10, 50, 30, 40, 20]
lst.sort()
print(lst[-2])

# Flatten a nested list
nested = [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]]
flat = [x for row in nested for x in row]
print(flat)

# Merge and sort two lists
a = [3, 1, 5]
b = [4, 2, 6]
merged = sorted(a + b)
print(merged)

# Find common elements
a = [1, 2, 3, 4]
b = [3, 4, 5, 6]
common = [x for x in a if x in b]
print(common)

# Rotate list by k positions
lst = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
k = 2
rotated = lst[k:] + lst[:k]
print(rotated)

important_notes: set() removes duplicates but does not preserve order — convert back to list with list(set(lst))
sorted() returns a new list; sort() modifies in place — choose based on whether you need the original
List comprehension is faster and more Pythonic than equivalent for loops
Always handle edge cases: check if list is empty before using max(), min(), or index()
subtopics.concept: List coding questions test the ability to combine indexing, slicing, loops, conditions, and methods to solve practical data manipulation problems.
subtopics.summary: List coding problems apply methods, comprehensions, and logic to solve tasks like finding max, filtering, removing duplicates, flattening, and merging lists.
real_time_examples: Finding top scorer from a list of marks
Removing duplicate entries from a contact list
Merging and sorting product lists from two stores

topics.title: Tuples
rules: Cannot modify

examples.code:
t=(1,2)
print(t)

important_notes: Faster than lists

subtopics.title: Tuple Basics
subtopics.definition: A tuple in Python is an ordered, immutable, and indexed collection that can store multiple items of different data types. Once created, its elements cannot be changed.
subtopics.flow: Define Tuple → Store in Variable → Access Elements → Use in Operations → Output
rules: Tuples are defined using parentheses ( )
Items are separated by commas
Tuples are immutable — elements cannot be added, removed, or changed after creation
A single-element tuple must have a trailing comma: (5,) not (5)
Tuples support indexing, slicing, and iteration just like lists
flowcharts: Start → Define Tuple with ( ) → Assign to Variable → Access Elements via Index → Use in Operations → Output → End

tables.title: Tuple Characteristics
rows: Same value can appear multiple times
Can be used as dictionary key

examples.code:
t = (1, 2, 3)
print(t)

t = ('apple', 'banana', 'cherry')
print(t[0])
print(t[-1])

t = (1, 'hello', 3.14, True)
print(t)
print(type(t))

# Single element tuple
t = (5,)
print(type(t))

t = (3, 1, 4, 1, 5)
print(t.count(1))
print(t.index(4))

# Tuple unpacking
t = (10, 20, 30)
a, b, c = t
print(a, b, c)

t = (1, 2, 3)
lst = list(t)
lst.append(4)
t = tuple(lst)
print(t)

important_notes: t = (5) is just an integer — always use t = (5,) for a single-element tuple
Tuples are faster than lists for read-only operations due to immutability
Tuple unpacking allows assigning multiple variables in one line: a, b = (1, 2)
To modify a tuple, convert to list → modify → convert back to tuple
subtopics.concept: Tuples are immutable ordered sequences — ideal for storing fixed data that should not change, such as coordinates, RGB values, or database records.
subtopics.summary: Tuples are ordered, immutable collections defined with ( ) that support indexing, slicing, unpacking, and mixed data types.
real_time_examples: Returning multiple values from a function

subtopics.title: Tuple Coding Questions
subtopics.definition: Practice problems that apply tuple properties and operations to solve real-world and logic-based Python programming challenges.
subtopics.flow: Understand Problem → Plan Logic → Write Code Using Tuple Operations → Test Output → Verify Result
rules: Remember tuples are immutable — convert to list if modification is needed
Use tuple unpacking to assign multiple variables cleanly
Use count() and index() — the only two built-in tuple methods
Use sorted() to sort a tuple — it returns a list, convert back with tuple()
Check for single-element tuples with trailing comma when creating dynamically
flowcharts: Start → Read Problem → Define Tuple → Apply Index / Loop / Unpacking / Conversion → Compute Result → Print Output → End

tables.title: Common Tuple Coding Patterns
rows: Check if item exists in tuple

examples.code:
# Find max and min from a tuple
t = (34, 78, 12, 56, 90)
print('Max:', max(t))
print('Min:', min(t))

# Count occurrences of an element
t = (1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 2, 5)
print('Count of 2:', t.count(2))

# Unpack tuple into variables
t = ('Alice', 25, 'Engineer')
name, age, role = t
print(name, age, role)

# Sort a tuple
t = (5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2)
sorted_t = tuple(sorted(t))
print(sorted_t)

# Convert tuple to list, add element, convert back
t = (1, 2, 3)
lst = list(t)
lst.append(4)
t = tuple(lst)
print(t)

# Merge two tuples
t1 = (1, 2, 3)
t2 = (4, 5, 6)
merged = t1 + t2
print(merged)

# Find index of an element
t = (10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
print('Index of 30:', t.index(30))

# Check membership in tuple
t = ('apple', 'banana', 'cherry')
fruit = 'banana'
if fruit in t:
    print(fruit, 'found at index', t.index(fruit))

# Sum and average of tuple elements
t = (10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
total = sum(t)
avg = total / len(t)
print('Sum:', total)
print('Average:', avg)

important_notes: Tuples have only two methods: count() and index() — all other operations use built-in functions
sorted() on a tuple returns a list — wrap with tuple() to keep it as a tuple
Tuple unpacking fails if the number of variables does not match the number of elements
Use * in unpacking to capture remaining elements: a, *b = (1, 2, 3, 4) → a=1, b=[2,3,4]
subtopics.concept: Tuple coding questions test the ability to use indexing, unpacking, conversion, and built-in functions to process fixed, ordered data efficiently.
subtopics.summary: Tuple coding problems apply count, index, unpacking, sorting, merging, and conversion techniques to solve practical data challenges with immutable sequences.
real_time_examples: Processing fixed student records (name, age, grade)
Handling API responses returned as tuples
Working with coordinate pairs in geometry problems

topics.title: Sets
rules: No duplicates

examples.code:
s={1,2,2}
print(s)

important_notes: Fast operations

subtopics.title: Set Basics
subtopics.definition: A set in Python is an unordered, mutable, and unindexed collection that stores only unique elements. Sets automatically eliminate duplicate values.
subtopics.flow: Define Set → Store in Variable → Add/Remove Elements → Perform Set Operations → Output
rules: Sets are defined using curly braces { } or the set() constructor
Sets do not allow duplicate values — duplicates are automatically removed
Sets are unordered — elements have no fixed position or index
Sets are mutable — elements can be added or removed after creation
Set elements must be immutable (hashable) — no lists or dicts as elements
flowcharts: Start → Define Set with { } → Assign to Variable → Add/Remove/Access Elements → Apply Set Operation → Output → End

tables.title: Set Characteristics
rows: No lists or dicts inside set

tables.title: Set vs List vs Tuple vs Dictionary

examples.code:
# Creating set using set()
s = set([10, 20, 30, 20])
print(s)

s = {'apple', 'banana', 'cherry'}
print('banana' in s)

s = {1, 2, 3}
print(len(s))

# Convert list to set to remove duplicates
lst = [1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3]
unique = set(lst)
print(unique)

important_notes: { } creates an empty dictionary — always use set() to create an empty set
Sets are unordered — the print order may differ every time
Sets cannot contain mutable elements like lists or other sets
frozenset() creates an immutable version of a set that can be used as a dictionary key
subtopics.concept: Sets are unordered collections of unique hashable elements — ideal for membership testing, removing duplicates, and mathematical set operations.
subtopics.summary: Sets are unordered, mutable, unique-element collections defined with { } or set() that do not support indexing or duplicates.
real_time_examples: Removing duplicate entries from a database
Finding common friends between two users
Tracking unique visitors to a website

subtopics.title: Set Methods
subtopics.definition: Set methods are built-in functions that operate on sets to add, remove, or perform mathematical set operations like union, intersection, and difference.
subtopics.flow: Define Set(s) → Call Method using Dot Notation → Set is Modified or New Set Returned → Output
rules: Set methods are called using dot notation: set.method()
Methods that modify the set return None (add, remove, discard, update)
Methods that return a new set do not modify the originals (union, intersection, difference)
discard() does not raise an error if element is missing; remove() does
Operator shortcuts: | for union, & for intersection, - for difference, ^ for symmetric difference
flowcharts: Start → Define One or More Sets → Call Method → Method Modifies Set or Returns New Set → Print / Use Result → End

tables.title: Set Methods Reference
rows: Adds element x to the set
Removes x; raises KeyError if not found
Removes x; no error if not found
Removes and returns an arbitrary element
Returns all elements from both sets
Returns elements in s1 not in s2
Returns elements in either but not both
True if s1 is subset of s2
True if s1 contains all of s2
Adds all elements of s2 into s1

important_notes: remove() raises KeyError if element not found — use discard() for safe removal
union(), intersection(), difference() return new sets — originals are unchanged
update() modifies the original set in place — equivalent to |=
pop() removes an arbitrary element — do not rely on it for ordered removal
subtopics.concept: Set methods provide powerful tools for managing unique collections and performing mathematical set operations like union, intersection, and difference efficiently.
subtopics.summary: Set methods (add, remove, discard, union, intersection, difference, symmetric_difference, issubset, issuperset) manage and compare sets using dot notation.
real_time_examples: Finding common skills between two candidates (intersection)
Combining unique tags from two articles (union)
Finding items discontinued from old menu (difference)

subtopics.title: Hash-Set Process
subtopics.definition: A hash-set is the internal mechanism Python uses to store set elements. Each element is passed through a hash function to compute a unique hash value that determines where it is stored in memory.
subtopics.flow: Element → Hash Function → Hash Value → Map to Bucket → Store in Memory → Retrieve in O(1)
rules: Every element in a set must be hashable (immutable)
Python uses hash() to compute an integer hash value for each element
Two equal objects always produce the same hash value
Hash collisions (different elements with same hash) are handled internally
Lookup, insertion, and deletion in a set are O(1) average time complexity
flowcharts: Start → Receive Element → Call hash(element) → Compute Bucket Index → Check Bucket → Empty? → Store | Occupied? → Check Equality → Match: Skip (duplicate) | No Match: Handle Collision → End

tables.title: Hash-Set Internal Process
rows: Element to be added is passed to the set
Python computes hash value: e.g. hash(42) = 42
Hash value is mapped to a memory bucket/slot
Is the bucket empty or occupied?
Store element directly in that bucket
Check if existing element equals new one
Same hash → same bucket → O(1) lookup

examples.code:
# hash() of common values
print(hash(42))
print(hash('hello'))
print(hash((1,2,3)))

# Duplicate detection via hash
s = set()
s.add(10)
s.add(10)  # Same hash — not added
print(s)

# frozenset is hashable — can be used as dict key
fs = frozenset([1,2,3])
d = {fs: 'triangle'}
print(d[fs])

# Hash consistency
print(hash('python') == hash('python'))

# Speed of set lookup vs list
import time
lst = list(range(1000000))
s = set(range(1000000))
print(999999 in s)  # O(1) — instant

# Two equal values have same hash
print(hash(1) == hash(1.0))
print(1 == 1.0)

important_notes: hash() values for strings change every Python session due to hash randomization (security feature)
Two objects that are equal (==) must have the same hash — but same hash does not mean equal (collision)
Sets achieve O(1) average time for add, remove, and lookup due to hashing
Making a custom class hashable requires implementing both __hash__() and __eq__()
subtopics.concept: Python sets use a hash table internally — each element is hashed to a memory bucket, enabling O(1) average-time insertion, deletion, and lookup while enforcing uniqueness.
subtopics.summary: Hash-sets use hash() to map elements to memory buckets, enabling O(1) operations and automatic duplicate detection through hash value comparison.
real_time_examples: O(1) username availability check in registration systems
Duplicate URL filtering in web crawlers
Fast membership testing in firewall rule engines

subtopics.title: Set Coding Questions
subtopics.definition: Practice problems that apply set properties, methods, and hash-set concepts to solve real-world and logic-based Python programming challenges.
subtopics.flow: Understand Problem → Plan Logic → Write Code Using Set Operations → Test Output → Verify Result
rules: Use set() to remove duplicates from any iterable instantly
Use intersection() for common elements, union() for combined, difference() for exclusive
Remember sets are unordered — do not rely on element order
Convert to sorted list if ordered output is needed
Use frozenset() when a set needs to be used as a dictionary key
flowcharts: Start → Read Problem → Define Set(s) → Apply Set Method / Operation / Loop → Compute Result → Print Output → End

tables.title: Common Set Coding Patterns
rows: Check if group A is part of group B

examples.code:
# Remove duplicates from a list
lst = [1, 2, 3, 2, 4, 3, 5]
unique = list(set(lst))
print(sorted(unique))

# Find common elements between two lists
a = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
b = [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]
common = set(a) & set(b)
print(common)

# Count unique characters in a string
s = 'programming'
print('Unique chars:', len(set(s)))

# Find words that appear in both sentences
s1 = set('the quick brown fox'.split())
s2 = set('the fox jumped high'.split())
print(s1 & s2)

# Find unique elements from two lists combined
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [3, 4, 5]
all_unique = set(a) | set(b)
print(sorted(all_unique))

# Remove duplicate emails from a list
emails = ['a@x.com','b@x.com','a@x.com','c@x.com']
unique_emails = list(set(emails))
print(len(unique_emails), 'unique emails')

important_notes: set() on a string iterates over characters — use split() first for words
Sets are unordered — wrap with sorted() when consistent output order is needed
Use & | - ^ operators for quick one-line set operations in coding problems
Converting list to set and back is the fastest way to remove duplicates in Python
subtopics.concept: Set coding questions test the ability to leverage uniqueness, O(1) lookup, and mathematical set operations to solve problems involving duplicates, membership, and group comparisons.
subtopics.summary: Set coding problems apply union, intersection, difference, symmetric difference, subset checks, and duplicate removal to solve practical data challenges efficiently.
real_time_examples: Finding mutual friends on a social platform
Detecting duplicate transactions in a payment system
Comparing skill sets of job applicants against requirements

topics.title: Dictionaries
rules: Keys must be unique

examples.code:
d={'a':1}
print(d['a'])

important_notes: Fast lookup

subtopics.title: Dictionary Basics
subtopics.definition: A dictionary in Python is an ordered, mutable, and indexed collection that stores data as key-value pairs. Each key must be unique and immutable, while values can be of any data type.
subtopics.flow: Define Dictionary → Store Key-Value Pairs → Access Value by Key → Modify / Add / Delete → Output
rules: Dictionaries are defined using curly braces { } with key:value pairs
Keys must be unique — duplicate keys overwrite the previous value
Keys must be immutable (strings, integers, tuples) — lists cannot be keys
Values can be of any data type including lists, tuples, or other dictionaries
Dictionaries are ordered as of Python 3.7 — insertion order is maintained
flowcharts: Start → Define Dictionary with { key:value } → Assign to Variable → Access Value via Key → Modify/Add/Delete Pairs → Output → End

tables.title: Dictionary Characteristics
rows: Values accessed via keys not positions
Values can be any Python object

examples.code:
d = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
print(d)

d = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
print(d['name'])

# Adding a new key-value pair
d = {'name': 'Alice'}
d['city'] = 'Paris'
print(d)

# Updating an existing value
d = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
d['age'] = 30
print(d)

# Deleting a key-value pair
d = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 25}
del d['age']
print(d)

# Check if key exists
d = {'a': 1, 'b': 2}
print('a' in d)
print('c' in d)

important_notes: Accessing a key that does not exist raises KeyError — use get() for safe access
Duplicate keys are silently overwritten — {'a':1,'a':2} results in {'a':2}
Nested dictionaries are accessed using chained keys: d['key1']['key2']
As of Python 3.7+, dictionaries maintain insertion order
subtopics.concept: Dictionaries are key-value mapping structures that allow fast O(1) access to values using unique keys — ideal for structured, labeled data storage.
subtopics.summary: Dictionaries are ordered, mutable key-value collections defined with { } that allow unique keys, mixed value types, and fast key-based access.
real_time_examples: Storing user profile data (name, age, email)
Mapping product IDs to prices in an e-commerce app
Counting word frequencies in a text document

subtopics.title: Dictionary Methods
subtopics.definition: Dictionary methods are built-in functions that operate on dictionaries to access, modify, remove, or iterate over key-value pairs efficiently.
subtopics.flow: Define Dictionary → Call Method using Dot Notation → Dictionary is Modified or Value Returned → Output
rules: Dictionary methods are called using dot notation: dict.method()
get() returns None instead of raising KeyError for missing keys
keys(), values(), items() return view objects — they reflect live changes
update() merges another dictionary or iterable of key-value pairs
pop() removes and returns a value; popitem() removes the last inserted pair
flowcharts: Start → Define Dictionary → Call Method → Method Modifies Dict or Returns Value/View → Print / Use Result → End

tables.title: Dictionary Methods Reference
rows: Returns value for key; default if not found
Returns all key-value pairs as tuples
Removes key and returns its value
Removes and returns last inserted pair
Returns value if key exists; else inserts key with val
Returns a shallow copy of the dictionary
Creates dict from sequence of keys with same value

tables.title: get() vs Direct Access
rows: When key is guaranteed to exist

examples.code:
d = {'name':'Alice','age':25}
removed = d.pop('age')
print(removed)
print(d)

d = {'a':1,'b':2,'c':3}
last = d.popitem()
print(last)
print(d)

d = {'a':1,'b':2}
d.setdefault('c',99)
d.setdefault('a',100)  # Won't overwrite
print(d)

d = {'a':1,'b':2}
copy_d = d.copy()
copy_d['c'] = 3
print(d)
print(copy_d)

keys = ['name','age','city']
d = dict.fromkeys(keys,'N/A')
print(d)

important_notes: keys(), values(), items() return views — not lists. Wrap with list() to get a list
pop() raises KeyError if key not found and no default is provided
update() overwrites existing keys with new values from the merged dictionary
setdefault() is useful for initializing keys without overwriting existing values
subtopics.concept: Dictionary methods provide efficient tools to safely access, iterate, merge, and manage key-value pairs without raising errors or losing data.
subtopics.summary: Dictionary methods (get, keys, values, items, update, pop, popitem, setdefault, copy, clear, fromkeys) manage key-value data using dot notation.
real_time_examples: Safe config value retrieval with get()

subtopics.title: Hash-Mapping Process
subtopics.definition: Hash-mapping is the internal mechanism Python uses to store and retrieve dictionary key-value pairs. Each key is passed through a hash function to compute a unique hash value that determines where its value is stored in memory.
subtopics.flow: Key → hash() Function → Hash Value → Map to Bucket Index → Store Value at Bucket → Retrieve in O(1)
rules: Dictionary keys must be hashable (immutable types only)
Python uses hash(key) to compute an integer that maps to a memory bucket
Two equal keys always produce the same hash value
Hash collisions are handled internally using open addressing or chaining
Dictionary lookup, insertion, and deletion are O(1) average time complexity
flowcharts: Start → Receive Key-Value Pair → Call hash(key) → Compute Bucket Index → Check Bucket → Empty? → Store Value | Occupied? → Same Key? → Update Value | Different Key? → Handle Collision → End

tables.title: Hash-Mapping Internal Process
rows: Is the slot empty or occupied?
Store key-value pair at that slot
hash(key) → same bucket → O(1) value access
Mark slot as deleted; maintain table integrity

tables.title: Hashable vs Non-Hashable Types as Keys

examples.code:
# hash() of common key types
print(hash('name'))
print(hash(42))
print(hash((1,2)))

# Duplicate key overwrites via same hash
d = {'a':1}
d['a'] = 99  # Same hash → same bucket → overwrite
print(d)

# hash consistency
print(hash('python') == hash('python'))  # Always True
print(hash(1) == hash(1.0))  # True — equal objects

# frozenset as dictionary key
fs = frozenset([1,2,3])
d = {fs: 'group A'}
print(d[fs])

important_notes: hash() values for strings are randomized per Python session for security — do not rely on exact values
True and 1 have the same hash — using both as keys results in only one entry
Dictionary resizing (rehashing) occurs automatically when load factor exceeds a threshold
Custom classes need __hash__() and __eq__() defined to be used as dictionary keys
subtopics.concept: Python dictionaries use a hash table internally — each key is hashed to a memory bucket, enabling O(1) average-time insertion, update, deletion, and lookup while enforcing key uniqueness.
subtopics.summary: Hash-mapping uses hash(key) to map keys to memory buckets, enabling O(1) dictionary operations and automatic key collision handling through probing.
real_time_examples: O(1) user session lookup by user ID
Caching computed results using input as key (memoization)
Database indexing using hash-based key mapping

subtopics.title: Dictionary Coding Questions
subtopics.definition: Practice problems that apply dictionary basics, methods, and hash-mapping concepts to solve real-world and logic-based Python programming challenges.
subtopics.flow: Understand Problem → Plan Logic → Write Code Using Dictionary Operations → Test Output → Verify Result
rules: Use get() for safe key access without risking KeyError
Use setdefault() or collections.defaultdict for building frequency maps
Use items() to iterate over key-value pairs simultaneously
Use dictionary comprehension for concise data transformation
Always check key existence with 'in' operator before direct access
flowcharts: Start → Read Problem → Define Dictionary → Apply Loop / Method / Comprehension → Compute Result → Print Output → End

tables.title: Dictionary Comprehension Patterns
rows: {k:v for k,v in d.items() if v>80}

examples.code:
# Count word frequency in a sentence
sentence = 'the cat sat on the mat the cat'
words = sentence.split()
freq = {}
for w in words:
    freq[w] = freq.get(w, 0) + 1
print(freq)

# Find key with maximum value
scores = {'Alice':85,'Bob':92,'Charlie':78}
top = max(scores, key=lambda x: scores[x])
print('Top scorer:', top)

# Sort dictionary by value
scores = {'Alice':85,'Bob':92,'Charlie':78,'Dave':95}
sorted_scores = dict(sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))
print(sorted_scores)

important_notes: freq.get(key, 0) + 1 is the standard pattern for building frequency maps without KeyError
sorted(d.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]) sorts by value — wrap with dict() to convert back
{**d1, **d2} is the most Pythonic way to merge two dictionaries (Python 3.5+)
collections.Counter is a specialized dictionary subclass for frequency counting
subtopics.concept: Dictionary coding questions test the ability to combine key-value access, iteration, comprehension, and methods to solve practical data mapping and frequency analysis problems.
subtopics.summary: Dictionary coding problems apply frequency counting, sorting, filtering, grouping, merging, and reversing techniques using dict methods and comprehensions.
real_time_examples: Word frequency analysis in text processing
Grouping orders by customer in an e-commerce system
Building an inverted index for a search engine

topics.title: Functions
rules: Define before use

examples.code: def add(a,b): return a+b

important_notes: Improves reuse

subtopics.title: Function Basics
subtopics.definition: A function is a reusable block of code that performs a specific task. It is defined once and can be called multiple times anywhere in the program.
subtopics.flow: Define Function → Call Function → Execute Body → Return Result (optional) → Continue Program
rules: Functions are defined using the def keyword followed by the function name and parentheses
Function names follow the same rules as variable names (lowercase, underscores)
The function body must be indented
A function must be defined before it is called
Functions can optionally return a value using the return statement
flowcharts: Start → def function_name(): → Write Function Body → Call function_name() → Execute Body → Return / None → Continue Program → End

tables.title: Function Structure
rows: Code executed when function is called

examples.code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(3, 5)
print(result)

def square(n):
    return n ** 2

print(square(4))
print(square(7))

def is_even(n):
    return n % 2 == 0

print(is_even(4))
print(is_even(7))

def multiply(a, b):
    """Returns product of a and b"""
    return a * b

print(multiply(4, 5))

examples.output:
Hi, Alice
Hi, Bob
Hi, Charlie

examples.code:
def calc(a, b):
    return a + b, a - b, a * b

result = calc(10, 3)
print(result)

important_notes: A function without return statement returns None by default
Functions are first-class objects in Python — they can be passed as arguments
Always use descriptive function names that explain what the function does
Docstrings (triple quotes) inside functions serve as documentation
subtopics.concept: Functions are reusable, named blocks of code that accept inputs (parameters), perform a task, and optionally return an output — promoting code reusability and modularity.
subtopics.summary: Functions are defined with def, called by name, accept parameters, execute a body, and optionally return a value.
real_time_examples: Send email function in notification systems

subtopics.title: Void and Non-Void Functions
subtopics.definition: A void function performs an action but does not return any value (returns None implicitly). A non-void function performs an action and explicitly returns a value using the return statement.
subtopics.flow: Void: Call → Execute Body → Print/Act → Return None | Non-Void: Call → Execute Body → Compute → Return Value → Use Result
rules: Void functions use print() to show output but return None
Non-void functions use return to send a value back to the caller
A function with no return statement is implicitly void
The returned value from a non-void function can be stored in a variable
return immediately exits the function — code after it does not execute
flowcharts: Void: Start → Call Function → Execute Body → print() output → Return None → End | Non-Void: Start → Call Function → Execute Body → Compute Result → return value → Caller receives value → End

tables.title: Return Value Types in Non-Void Functions

examples.code:
# Void function
def greet(name):
    print(f'Hello, {name}!')

greet('Alice')

# Void function returns None
def show(x):
    print(x)

result = show(10)
print(result)

# Non-void function
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

result = add(5, 3)
print(result)

# Non-void returning boolean
def is_adult(age):
    return age >= 18

print(is_adult(20))
print(is_adult(15))

# Non-void returning string
def full_name(first, last):
    return first + ' ' + last

name = full_name('John', 'Doe')
print(name)

# Non-void returning list
def squares(n):
    return [i**2 for i in range(1, n+1)]

print(squares(5))

# Early return in non-void
def divide(a, b):
    if b == 0:
        return 'Cannot divide by zero'
    return a / b

print(divide(10, 2))
print(divide(5, 0))

# Void — writing to file (simulated)
def log_message(msg):
    print(f'[LOG]: {msg}')

log_message('User logged in')
log_message('File saved')

# Using non-void result in expression
def tax(amount):
    return amount * 0.18

total = 500 + tax(500)
print('Total:', total)

important_notes: Never use the return value of a void function — it is always None
return exits the function immediately — any code after it in the same block is unreachable
A function can have multiple return statements (e.g., inside if-else)
Non-void functions are preferred when the result needs to be reused or tested
subtopics.concept: Void functions perform actions without returning a value; non-void functions compute and return a result that the caller can store, print, or use in further expressions.
subtopics.summary: Void functions print/act and return None; non-void functions use return to send back a value that can be stored and reused.

subtopics.title: Callback Functions
subtopics.definition: A callback function is a function passed as an argument to another function and called (executed) inside that function at a specific point.
subtopics.flow: Define Callback Function → Pass as Argument → Receiving Function Calls It Internally → Callback Executes → Result Returned
rules: Functions in Python are first-class objects — they can be passed as arguments
Pass the function name without parentheses when passing as a callback
The receiving function decides when and how many times to call the callback
Callbacks can be named functions, lambda functions, or built-in functions
Callbacks enable flexible and reusable function design
flowcharts: Start → Define callback() → Define main(func) → Call main(callback) → Inside main: func() is called → callback executes → Result → End

examples.code:
# Callback with map()
def square(x):
    return x ** 2

result = list(map(square, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))
print(result)

# Callback with filter()
def is_even(x):
    return x % 2 == 0

result = list(filter(is_even, [1,2,3,4,5,6]))
print(result)

# Callback with sorted()
words = ['banana', 'apple', 'kiwi', 'cherry']
sorted_words = sorted(words, key=len)
print(sorted_words)

# Callback to transform data
def transform(data, func):
    return [func(x) for x in data]

print(transform([1,2,3,4], str))
print(transform(['a','b','c'], str.upper))

important_notes: Pass function name without () — greet not greet() — otherwise you pass the result, not the function
map() and filter() return iterators — wrap with list() to see all results
Callbacks make code flexible — the same higher-order function can behave differently based on the callback passed
Lambda functions are commonly used as inline callbacks for simple one-time operations
subtopics.concept: Callback functions enable higher-order programming — passing behavior (functions) as data, making code flexible, reusable, and event-driven.
subtopics.summary: A callback is a function passed as an argument to another function and called inside it — enabling flexible, reusable, and event-driven code.
real_time_examples: onClick event handler in web apps
Sorting products by price or name using key= callback
map() to apply discount to all prices in a list

subtopics.title: Function Arguments
subtopics.definition: Arguments are values passed to a function when it is called. Python supports positional, keyword, default, and variable-length arguments for flexible function design.
subtopics.flow: Define Function with Parameters → Call with Arguments → Python Maps Arguments to Parameters → Execute Body → Return Result
rules: Positional arguments must be passed in the correct order
Keyword arguments are passed by parameter name — order does not matter
Default arguments have a preset value used when no argument is provided
Default arguments must come after non-default arguments in the definition
You can mix positional and keyword arguments in a call
flowcharts: Start → Function Called with Arguments → Positional? → Map by Order | Keyword? → Map by Name | Default? → Use Preset Value → Execute Body → Return → End

tables.title: Types of Function Arguments
rows: Collects extra positional args as tuple
Collects extra keyword args as dict

examples.code:
# Positional arguments
def add(a, b):
    return a + b

print(add(3, 7))

# Keyword arguments
def describe(name, age):
    print(f'{name} is {age} years old')

describe(age=25, name='Alice')

# Default arguments
def greet(name, msg='Hello'):
    print(msg + ',', name)

greet('Alice')
greet('Bob', 'Hi')

# Default argument — not passed
def power(base, exp=2):
    return base ** exp

print(power(3))
print(power(3, 3))

# Mix of positional and keyword
def order(item, qty, price):
    print(f'{qty} x {item} = ₹{qty*price}')

order('Apple', price=10, qty=5)

# Multiple default arguments
def register(name, age=18, city='Unknown'):
    print(f'{name}, {age}, {city}')

register('Alice')
register('Bob', 25)
register('Charlie', 30, 'Delhi')
examples.output:
Alice, 18, Unknown
Bob, 25, Unknown
Charlie, 30, Delhi

examples.code:
# Keyword arguments — any order
def box(length, width, height):
    return length * width * height

print(box(height=3, length=5, width=4))

# Positional argument mismatch error
def sub(a, b):
    return a - b

print(sub(10, 3))
print(sub(3, 10))

# Default mutable argument — use None pattern
def add_item(item, lst=None):
    if lst is None:
        lst = []
    lst.append(item)
    return lst

print(add_item('a'))
print(add_item('b'))

# Combining all types
def info(name, age=18, *hobbies):
    print(name, age, hobbies)

info('Alice', 25, 'chess', 'reading')

important_notes: Never use mutable objects (list, dict) as default argument values — use None and initialize inside the function
Positional arguments must always come before keyword arguments in a function call
Default argument values are evaluated once at function definition time, not at call time
Keyword arguments improve readability, especially in functions with many parameters
subtopics.concept: Python's flexible argument system allows functions to be called with positional order, named keywords, preset defaults, or variable-length inputs — enabling clean, adaptable APIs.
subtopics.summary: Python supports positional, keyword, default, and variable-length arguments for flexible and readable function calls.
real_time_examples: Keyword args in database query functions
Default timeout=30 in HTTP request functions

subtopics.title: *args and **kwargs
subtopics.definition: *args allows a function to accept any number of positional arguments as a tuple. **kwargs allows a function to accept any number of keyword arguments as a dictionary.
subtopics.flow: Call with Many Args → *args collects positional into tuple | **kwargs collects keyword into dict → Iterate or Access Inside Function → Process → Return
rules: *args collects extra positional arguments into a tuple
**kwargs collects extra keyword arguments into a dictionary
The names args and kwargs are conventions — any name with * or ** works
Order in definition: regular params → *args → keyword-only → **kwargs
*args and **kwargs can be used together in the same function
flowcharts: Start → Function Called → Regular args mapped first → Extra positional → *args tuple → Extra keyword → **kwargs dict → Process inside function → Return → End

examples.output:
Hello Alice
Hello Bob
Hello Charlie

examples.code:
# *args and **kwargs together
def mixed(*args, **kwargs):
    print('Args:', args)
    print('Kwargs:', kwargs)

mixed(1, 2, 3, name='Alice', age=25)

# Unpacking list into *args
def add(a, b, c):
    return a + b + c

nums = [1, 2, 3]
print(add(*nums))

# Full example: regular + *args + **kwargs
def order(store, *items, **details):
    print('Store:', store)
    print('Items:', items)
    print('Details:', details)

order('BigMart', 'Apple', 'Milk', qty=2, paid=True)

important_notes: *args is a tuple inside the function — use indexing or iteration to access elements
**kwargs is a dictionary inside the function — use .items() to iterate
Use * to unpack a list into positional args and ** to unpack a dict into keyword args when calling
Keyword-only parameters can be defined after *args: def f(*args, key=val)
subtopics.concept: *args and **kwargs provide ultimate flexibility for functions that need to accept an unknown number of inputs — enabling generic, reusable utility functions.
subtopics.summary: *args collects extra positional arguments as a tuple; **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments as a dictionary — both enable variable-length function inputs.
real_time_examples: print() uses *args to accept any number of values
Django/Flask routes use **kwargs for URL parameters
Logging functions use **kwargs for flexible metadata

subtopics.title: Python Scope
subtopics.definition: Scope defines the region of code where a variable is accessible. Python follows the LEGB rule: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in, searching each scope in order.
subtopics.flow: Variable Access → Search Local Scope → Not Found? → Search Enclosing → Not Found? → Search Global → Not Found? → Search Built-in → Not Found? → NameError
rules: Local scope: variables defined inside a function — only accessible within it
Enclosing scope: variables in the outer function of a nested function
Global scope: variables defined at the top level of a module
Built-in scope: Python's built-in names like print, len, range
Use global keyword to modify a global variable inside a function
Use nonlocal keyword to modify an enclosing variable inside a nested function
flowcharts: Variable Lookup: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in → NameError if not found

examples.code:
# Local shadows global
x = 50

def func():
    x = 99  # local x
    print('Inside:', x)

func()
print('Outside:', x)

# global keyword
count = 0

def increment():
    global count
    count += 1

increment()
increment()
print(count)

# Enclosing scope (closure)
def outer():
    x = 10
    def inner():
        print('Inner sees x:', x)
    inner()

outer()

# nonlocal keyword
def outer():
    x = 10
    def inner():
        nonlocal x
        x = 99
    inner()
    print('After inner:', x)

outer()

# Built-in scope
print(len('Python'))  # len is built-in
print(type(42))       # type is built-in

# LEGB in action
x = 'global'

def outer():
    x = 'enclosing'
    def inner():
        x = 'local'
        print(x)
    inner()
    print(x)

outer()
print(x)

# global counter pattern
total = 0

def add(n):
    global total
    total += n

add(10)
add(20)
add(30)
print('Total:', total)

# Closure — enclosing scope preserved
def make_multiplier(n):
    def multiplier(x):
        return x * n  # n from enclosing scope
    return multiplier

double = make_multiplier(2)
triple = make_multiplier(3)
print(double(5))
print(triple(5))

important_notes: Python searches scopes in LEGB order — Local first, Built-in last
Avoid overwriting built-in names like list, print, len as variable names
global should be used sparingly — it makes code harder to debug
Closures capture enclosing scope variables even after the outer function has returned
subtopics.concept: Python's LEGB scope rule defines a strict lookup order for variable names — ensuring predictable access and preventing naming conflicts across local, enclosing, global, and built-in namespaces.
subtopics.summary: Python uses LEGB scope: Local → Enclosing → Global → Built-in. Use global to modify globals and nonlocal for enclosing scope inside nested functions.
real_time_examples: Global config variables shared across functions

subtopics.title: Python Decorators
subtopics.definition: A decorator is a function that takes another function as input, adds extra functionality to it, and returns a new enhanced function — without modifying the original function's code.
subtopics.flow: Define Decorator → Define Wrapper Inside → Add Extra Code → Return Wrapper → Apply with @decorator → Call Function → Extra Code Runs → Original Function Runs
rules: Decorators are applied using the @decorator_name syntax above a function
A decorator takes a function as argument and returns a new function (wrapper)
The wrapper function adds behavior before and/or after the original function
Multiple decorators can be stacked — applied bottom-up
Use functools.wraps to preserve the original function's metadata
flowcharts: Start → @decorator applied → Decorator receives func → Defines wrapper() → wrapper adds extra behavior → wrapper calls original func() → Returns wrapper → Calling func() now calls wrapper() → End

examples.output:
Welcome to Dashboard!
Access denied! Please login.

important_notes: @decorator is syntactic sugar for func = decorator(func)
Always use *args and **kwargs in wrapper to handle functions with any arguments
Use functools.wraps(func) to preserve __name__ and __doc__ of the original function
Stacked decorators apply bottom-up: @bold @italic applies italic first, then bold
subtopics.concept: Decorators implement the wrapper pattern — adding cross-cutting concerns (logging, timing, auth) around functions without modifying their source code, following the Open/Closed principle.
subtopics.summary: Decorators wrap functions to add extra behavior using @syntax. They take a function, return a wrapper, and are used for logging, timing, auth, and validation.

subtopics.title: Python Lambda
subtopics.definition: A lambda function is a small, anonymous (nameless) function defined in a single line using the lambda keyword. It can take any number of arguments but contains only one expression.
subtopics.flow: lambda keyword → Parameters → Colon → Single Expression → Returns Result Automatically
rules: Syntax: lambda parameters: expression
Lambda functions are anonymous — they have no name unless assigned to a variable
They can only contain a single expression — no statements, no return keyword
The expression result is automatically returned
Lambdas are commonly used as inline callbacks with map(), filter(), sorted()
flowcharts: Start → lambda params: expression → Evaluate Expression → Return Result Automatically → End

examples.code:
# Basic lambda
add = lambda a, b: a + b
print(add(3, 5))

# Lambda with single argument
square = lambda x: x ** 2
print(square(6))

# Lambda with condition
check = lambda x: 'Even' if x % 2 == 0 else 'Odd'
print(check(4))
print(check(7))

# Lambda with map()
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
doubled = list(map(lambda x: x * 2, nums))
print(doubled)

# Lambda with filter()
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
evens = list(filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, nums))
print(evens)

# Lambda with sorted()
students = [('Alice', 85), ('Bob', 92), ('Charlie', 78)]
sorted_students = sorted(students, key=lambda x: x[1])
print(sorted_students)

# Lambda with max()
people = [{'name':'Alice','age':30},{'name':'Bob','age':25}]
oldest = max(people, key=lambda p: p['age'])
print(oldest['name'])

# Lambda inside function (factory)
def make_multiplier(n):
    return lambda x: x * n

triple = make_multiplier(3)
print(triple(5))
print(triple(10))

# Immediately invoked lambda
result = (lambda x, y: x + y)(10, 20)
print(result)

# Lambda with multiple map operations
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
result = list(map(lambda x: x**2 + 1, nums))
print(result)

important_notes: Lambda functions should be short and simple — use def for anything complex
Lambda cannot contain statements like print, if-else blocks, or loops (only expressions)
The ternary expression lambda x: 'A' if x>90 else 'B' is valid since it is an expression
Avoid assigning lambdas to variables for reuse — use def instead for named reusable functions
subtopics.concept: Lambda functions are concise anonymous functions for simple one-expression operations — ideal as inline callbacks for map(), filter(), sorted(), and other higher-order functions.
subtopics.summary: Lambda creates anonymous single-expression functions using lambda params: expression — commonly used with map(), filter(), sorted(), and as inline callbacks.
real_time_examples: Sorting products by price in one line
Filtering active users from a list
Transforming API response fields with map()

subtopics.title: Python Recursion
subtopics.definition: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller sub-problems of the same type. Every recursive function must have a base case to stop the recursion.
subtopics.flow: Call Function → Check Base Case → If Yes: Return Result → If No: Call Itself with Smaller Input → Build Up Results → Final Answer
rules: Every recursive function must have a base case that stops the recursion
Each recursive call must move closer to the base case
Without a base case, recursion causes infinite calls and RecursionError
Python's default recursion limit is 1000 — use sys.setrecursionlimit() to change
Recursion uses the call stack — deep recursion can cause stack overflow
flowcharts: Start → Call func(n) → Is base case? → Yes: return base value → No: return func(n-1) or smaller → Stack unwinds → Final Result → End

examples.code:
# Factorial
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:  # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n - 1)

print(factorial(5))
print(factorial(0))

# Count down
def countdown(n):
    if n == 0:
        print('Go!')
        return
    print(n)
    countdown(n - 1)

countdown(5)

important_notes: Always define the base case first to prevent infinite recursion
Python raises RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded after ~1000 calls
Memoization (caching results) greatly improves recursive performance for overlapping subproblems
Tail recursion is not optimized in Python — iterative solutions are preferred for performance-critical code
subtopics.concept: Recursion breaks a problem into smaller identical sub-problems, solving the smallest case (base case) directly and combining results as the call stack unwinds.
subtopics.summary: Recursion is a function calling itself with a smaller input until a base case is reached — used for factorial, fibonacci, tree traversal, and divide-and-conquer problems.

subtopics.title: Python Generators
subtopics.definition: A generator is a special type of function that yields values one at a time using the yield keyword instead of returning all values at once. It pauses execution after each yield and resumes from the same point when next() is called.
subtopics.flow: Define Generator with yield → Call Function → Get Generator Object → Call next() → Execute Until yield → Pause → Call next() Again → Resume → StopIteration when Done
rules: Use yield instead of return to make a function a generator
Generators produce values lazily — one at a time, only when requested
Calling a generator function returns a generator object, not a value
Use next() to get the next value or a for loop to iterate all values
Generators raise StopIteration automatically when the function ends
flowcharts: Start → Call generator_func() → Returns generator object → Call next() → Execute until yield → Yield value → Pause → Call next() again → Resume from yield → No more yields → StopIteration → End

tables.title: Generator vs Regular Function
rows: Pauses at yield, resumes on next()
O(1) — one value at a time

examples.code:
# Using next() manually
def gen():
    yield 'A'
    yield 'B'
    yield 'C'

g = gen()
print(next(g))
print(next(g))
print(next(g))

# Generator expression
gen = (x**2 for x in range(1, 6))
for val in gen:
    print(val, end=' ')

# Generator with send()
def accumulator():
    total = 0
    while True:
        value = yield total
        if value is None:
            break
        total += value

g = accumulator()
next(g)
print(g.send(10))
print(g.send(20))
print(g.send(30))

# StopIteration demonstration
def two_items():
    yield 1
    yield 2

g = two_items()
print(next(g))
print(next(g))
try:
    print(next(g))
except StopIteration:
    print('Generator exhausted!')

important_notes: yield pauses the function and saves its state — next() resumes from the exact same point
Generator expressions (x**2 for x in range(n)) are memory-efficient alternatives to list comprehensions
Once a generator is exhausted, it cannot be restarted — create a new generator object
Generators are ideal for processing large datasets, streams, or infinite sequences without memory overflow
subtopics.concept: Generators use yield to produce values lazily one at a time — pausing execution between yields — enabling memory-efficient iteration over large or infinite sequences.
subtopics.summary: Generators use yield to produce values one at a time on demand. They pause between yields, preserve state, and are memory-efficient for large or infinite data.
real_time_examples: Streaming large CSV files row by row
Generating infinite prime numbers on demand
Building data processing pipelines with chained generators

subtopics.flow: Class (Blueprint) → Object (Instance) → Access Attributes/Methods → Output
rules: Class name starts with uppercase (PascalCase)
Objects are created using className()
Each object has its own memory space

subtopics.definition: __init__ is a special method (constructor) called automatically when an object is created. It initializes the object's attributes.
subtopics.flow: Create Object → __init__ called automatically → Attributes set → Object ready to use
rules: __init__ is always the first method called
self is mandatory as the first parameter
Default values can be given to parameters
A class can have only one __init__
__init__ does not return any value
flowcharts: obj = Class(args) → __init__(self, args) → self.attr = value → Object initialized

examples.code:
class Book:
    def __init__(self, title, author, pages):
        self.title = title

b = Book('Python', 'Guido', 500)

important_notes: __init__ runs automatically on object creation
It is not called manually
Used to set initial state of the object
subtopics.concept: Constructor sets up an object's initial data so it's ready to use the moment it's created.
subtopics.summary: __init__ is the starting point that gives every object its identity
real_time_examples: Opening a bank account fills in your name, ID, balance=0 — that's __init__
Booking a train ticket initializes passenger name, seat, destination
Creating a new user account sets username, password, date_joined automatically

subtopics.title: Abstraction
subtopics.definition: Abstraction hides internal implementation details and shows only the necessary interface to the user. In Python, achieved using Abstract Base Classes (ABC).
subtopics.flow: Define Abstract Class (ABC) → Declare abstract methods → Subclass inherits → Subclass must implement all abstract methods → Use subclass objects
rules: Import ABC and abstractmethod from abc module
Abstract class cannot be instantiated directly
All abstract methods must be overridden in subclass
Concrete methods can exist in abstract class
Use @abstractmethod decorator
flowcharts: from abc import ABC, abstractmethod → class Shape(ABC) → @abstractmethod def area() → class Circle(Shape) → def area(): return ... → c = Circle()
important_notes: You cannot instantiate an abstract class directly
Every abstract method must be implemented in child class
ABC forces a contract on all subclasses
subtopics.concept: Abstraction lets you define WHAT to do without specifying HOW — subclasses fill in the how.
subtopics.summary: Hide complexity, expose only what's necessary
real_time_examples: ATM machine: you press 'Withdraw', internal bank logic is hidden from you
Car steering: you turn the wheel, engine mechanics are abstracted away
Payment gateway: merchants call pay(), internal processing is hidden

subtopics.title: Encapsulation
subtopics.definition: Encapsulation is binding data (attributes) and methods together inside a class, and restricting direct access from outside using access modifiers.
subtopics.flow: Data + Methods bundled in class → Access modifiers applied → Outside code uses getters/setters → Data is protected
rules: public: no underscore, accessible everywhere
protected: single underscore (_), accessible in class + subclasses
private: double underscore (__), accessible only inside class
Use getter/setter methods to access private data
Name mangling: __var becomes _ClassName__var
flowcharts: Attribute → public (name) → anywhere accessible
Attribute → protected (_name) → class + subclass
Attribute → private (__name) → only inside class → use getter/setter
important_notes: Private variables cannot be directly accessed outside the class
Use getters and setters for controlled access
Encapsulation prevents accidental data modification
subtopics.concept: Encapsulation wraps data safely inside a class — like a capsule — so only authorized code can touch it.
subtopics.summary: Bundle data + methods; restrict direct access for safety
real_time_examples: Bank account: your balance is private; only the bank's methods can change it
Medical records: patient data is private; accessed only through authorized methods
Car dashboard: internal engine data is encapsulated; you see only speed/fuel gauges

subtopics.title: Inheritance
subtopics.definition: Inheritance allows a child class to acquire the properties and methods of a parent class, enabling code reuse and hierarchical relationships.
subtopics.flow: Parent Class (base) → Child Class (derived) inherits → Child adds/overrides → Object created from Child → Uses both parent + child features
rules: Use class Child(Parent) syntax
super() calls the parent class constructor/method
Child can override parent methods
Python supports single, multilevel, multiple, hierarchical, hybrid inheritance
isinstance() checks object's class chain
flowcharts: Single: Animal → Dog
Multilevel: Animal → Dog → Labrador
Multiple: FlyingAnimal + SwimmingAnimal → Duck
Hierarchical: Animal → Dog, Cat, Bird
Hybrid: combination of above types
important_notes: MRO (Method Resolution Order) determines method lookup in multiple/hybrid inheritance
super() always refers to the next class in MRO
Use __mro__ or mro() to see the resolution order
subtopics.concept: Inheritance allows child classes to reuse parent class features — just like children inherit traits from parents.
subtopics.summary: Reuse code from parent classes; extend or override as needed
real_time_examples: Single: Employee → Manager (manager is a type of employee)
Multilevel: Animal → Mammal → Human (chain of evolution)
Multiple: SmartPhone inherits from Phone + Camera + MusicPlayer
Hierarchical: Vehicle → Car, Bike, Bus, Truck
Hybrid: Indian University system — department → course → student specialization

subtopics.title: Polymorphism
subtopics.definition: Polymorphism means 'many forms'. The same method name behaves differently based on the object calling it or the arguments passed.
subtopics.flow: Same method name → Different classes or argument counts → Different behavior → Single interface, multiple implementations
rules: Method overriding: child redefines parent's method
Method overloading: same name, different behavior based on args (simulated in Python)
Duck typing: if it walks like a duck, Python treats it as a duck
Use *args to simulate overloading
super() can call parent method from overridden child method
flowcharts: Overriding: Parent.speak() → Child.speak() (different output)
Overloading: add(2,3) → 5 | add(2,3,4) → 9 (using *args)

examples.code:
# Overloading with default args
class Greeter:
    def greet(self, name='Guest', greeting='Hello'):
        return f'{greeting}, {name}!'

g = Greeter()
print(g.greet())
print(g.greet('Ravi'))
print(g.greet('Ravi', 'Namaste'))

important_notes: Python doesn't support true method overloading — use *args or default params
Overriding is fully supported — child method replaces parent's
Duck typing allows polymorphism without inheritance
subtopics.concept: Same interface, different behavior — polymorphism lets one name serve many purposes.
subtopics.summary: One name, many forms — behavior changes based on the object
real_time_examples: Overriding: Pay button — same button, different logic for GPay, Paytm, Card
Overloading: Calculator add button — adds 2 numbers or 3 numbers or decimals
Duck typing: Export button on any file type (PDF, Word, Excel) — same call

subtopics.title: Mangling
subtopics.definition: Name mangling is Python's mechanism to make a private attribute (with __prefix) harder to access outside the class by renaming it to _ClassName__attribute internally.
subtopics.flow: Define __attribute in class → Python renames to _ClassName__attribute → Direct access fails → Access via _ClassName__attribute works
rules: Double underscore prefix triggers mangling
Mangled name: __attr → _ClassName__attr
Still accessible via mangled name (not truly private)
Prevents accidental override in subclasses
Trailing double underscores (__attr__) are NOT mangled (dunder methods)
flowcharts: class Foo: __secret = 1 → internally stored as _Foo__secret → f.__secret fails → f._Foo__secret works

examples.code:
class APIClient:
    __api_key = 'sk-xxxx'

    def call(self):
        return f'Calling with {self.__api_key}'

client = APIClient()
print(client.call())
print(client._APIClient__api_key)

important_notes: Mangling is NOT true security — it just prevents accidental access
Useful to avoid name clashes in inheritance
Python philosophy: 'We're all consenting adults' — convention over enforcement
subtopics.concept: Name mangling renames __attr to _Class__attr so subclasses don't accidentally override parent private attributes.
subtopics.summary: Double underscore renames private attributes to avoid inheritance conflicts
real_time_examples: API key stored as __api_key in a service class — not directly accessible from subclasses
PIN stored as __pin in bank class — mangled to _Bank__pin internally
Internal config stored as __settings in framework code — protected from accidental override

subtopics.title: Inner Classes
subtopics.definition: An inner class (nested class) is a class defined inside another class. It helps group related classes together and controls access to the outer class's members.
subtopics.flow: Outer class defined → Inner class defined inside → Inner class accessed via Outer().Inner() or Outer.Inner() → Inner object created → Used independently or linked to outer
rules: Inner class is defined inside the outer class body
Access: obj = Outer(); inner = obj.Inner()
Inner class can access outer class attributes via outer object reference
Inner class does NOT automatically have access to outer class's self
Useful for helper classes, builder patterns, and logical grouping
flowcharts: class Outer: → class Inner: → outer_obj = Outer() → inner_obj = outer_obj.Inner() → inner_obj.method()
important_notes: Inner class does not have implicit access to outer class's self
Pass the outer object explicitly if inner class needs outer data
Can be used to implement Builder and Factory patterns
subtopics.concept: Inner classes group tightly related entities — like a Car containing an Engine class — keeping structure clean and logical.
subtopics.summary: Define classes inside classes for logical grouping and encapsulation
real_time_examples: Hospital system: HospitalManagement has inner classes Patient, Doctor, Ward
E-commerce: Order class contains inner Item class
University: University → Department → Course (nested inner classes)
